steps.message:
<permissions instructions>
Filesystem sandboxing defines which files can be read or written. `sandbox_mode` is `danger-full-access`: No filesystem sandboxing - all commands are permitted. Network access is enabled.
Approval policy is currently never. Do not provide the `sandbox_permissions` for any reason, commands will be rejected.
</permissions instructions><skills_instructions>
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- imagegen: Generate or edit raster images when the task benefits from AI-created bitmap visuals such as photos, illustrations, textures, sprites, mockups, or transparent-background cutouts. Use when Codex should create a brand-new image, transform an existing image, or derive visual variants from references, and the output should be a bitmap asset rather than repo-native code or vector. Do not use when the task is better handled by editing existing SVG/vector/code-native assets, extending an established icon or logo system, or building the visual directly in HTML/CSS/canvas. (file: /logs/agent/skills/.system/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or model upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /logs/agent/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- plugin-creator: Create and scaffold plugin directories for Codex with a required `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, optional plugin folders/files, and baseline placeholders you can edit before publishing or testing. Use when Codex needs to create a new local plugin, add optional plugin structure, or generate or update repo-root `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` entries for plugin ordering and availability metadata. (file: /logs/agent/skills/.system/plugin-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /logs/agent/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /logs/agent/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</skills_instructions>

Our `textd` deploy soak keeps dying during repeated in-place processor refreshes. The daemon is supposed to rotate customer-specific text processors without restarting the JVM, and basic request/response behavior still looks fine, but the long-running validation under the constrained CI runtime profile does not survive the full refresh sequence.

Treat this as a runtime lifecycle problem rather than a bad plugin payload. The service needs to complete the reload soak and keep returning the same deterministic text-processing results across processor generations and across the already-running workers that continue serving requests before and after each refresh.

I’m treating this as a processor lifecycle bug under repeated hot refresh, not a content issue. I’ll inspect the soak harness, reload path, and worker/processor ownership first, then patch the runtime and verify it under the constrained CI profile.

steps.message: The repo is small and not under git here, so I’m reading the hot-reload path directly. Next I’m checking the generation registry, daemon request path, and the soak runners to find where old workers outlive or lose the processor generation they started on.

The likely fault is already visible: worker threads keep a thread-local `DispatchBinding`, but refresh replaces it without closing the previous lease, so old generations stay pinned indefinitely. I’m confirming that against the build and soak scripts, then I’ll fix the binding swap and shutdown semantics so reused workers don’t leak generations.

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

ROOT_DIR=$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)
BUILD_DIR="$ROOT_DIR/build"
MAIN_CLASSES="$BUILD_DIR/main-classes"
PLUGIN_CLASSES="$BUILD_DIR/plugin-classes"
PLUGIN_DIR="$BUILD_DIR/plugins"

rm -rf "$MAIN_CLASSES" "$PLUGIN_CLASSES"
mkdir -p "$MAIN_CLASSES" "$PLUGIN_CLASSES" "$PLUGIN_DIR"

find "$ROOT_DIR/src/main/java" -name '*.java' | sort > "$BUILD_DIR/main-sources.txt"
javac --release 17 -d "$MAIN_CLASSES" @"$BUILD_DIR/main-sources.txt"

find "$ROOT_DIR/plugin-src" -name '*.java' | sort > "$BUILD_DIR/plugin-sources.txt"
javac --release 17 -cp "$MAIN_CLASSES" -d "$PLUGIN_CLASSES" @"$BUILD_DIR/plugin-sources.txt"

jar --create --file "$PLUGIN_DIR/customer-default.jar" -C "$PLUGIN_CLASSES" .

steps.message: I’ve confirmed the design: every generation builds a fresh class loader, so leaking even one binding per refresh will eventually exhaust metaspace under the soak. I’m reproducing the failure now, then I’ll patch the thread-local handoff so replaced bindings and shutdown paths actually release their leases.

steps.message: The immediate leak is definite, but there’s also a shutdown lifecycle gap: worker thread-local bindings never call `close()`, so even the final active generation can stay artificially referenced after daemon shutdown. I’m checking for any existing cleanup hooks, then I’ll patch both replacement and worker teardown together.

results.content:
Chunk ID: 00d81a
Wall time: 0.0000 seconds
Process exited with code 0
Original token count: 0
Output:

steps.message:
I have the fix shape now:
1. Acquire the active generation with a retained lease, so refresh and dispatch can’t race into a closed class loader.
2. Close the old thread-local binding when a worker switches generations.
3. Release any remaining worker binding when the pool threads terminate so shutdown fully drains retirements.

I’m patching those core lifecycle classes now, then I’ll rerun the soak and lifecycle probe.

steps.message: The core patch is in. I changed the generation handoff to use retained active leases, made worker swaps explicitly close the old binding, and wired worker thread teardown to release their final binding on exit. I’m compiling and running the suite now, and I’ll tighten the probe if the lifecycle counters still need explicit assertions.

steps.message: The runtime fix is holding under soak, but the suite still wouldn’t fail early if someone reintroduced the leak later because the lifecycle probe mostly prints JSON. I’m tightening the probe and stress runner with explicit lifecycle assertions so this stays a runtime regression test instead of relying on another eventual metaspace blowup.

steps.message:
The leak was in the worker-side generation binding, not in the plugin payload. Each refresh created a new class loader, but reused worker threads were replacing their thread-local `DispatchBinding` without closing the old lease, so retired generations stayed pinned until the 64 MB metaspace cap failed the soak. I fixed that by making active-generation acquisition retain-safe during reload races, closing the old binding on generation swap, and releasing the final worker binding when pool threads exit. See [GenerationRegistry.java](/workspace/textd/src/main/java/io/harbor/textd/core/GenerationRegistry.java:35), [GenerationLease.java](/workspace/textd/src/main/java/io/harbor/textd/core/GenerationLease.java:18), [WorkerBindings.java](/workspace/textd/src/main/java/io/harbor/textd/core/WorkerBindings.java:9), [DispatchBinding.java](/workspace/textd/src/main/java/io/harbor/textd/core/DispatchBinding.java:10), and [TextDaemon.java](/workspace/textd/src/main/java/io/harbor/textd/core/TextDaemon.java:93).

I also tightened the regression coverage so the lifecycle checks fail immediately if retirements accumulate or worker reuse breaks, instead of waiting for another metaspace OOM. See [HotReloadLifecycleProbe.java](/workspace/textd/src/main/java/io/harbor/textd/cli/HotReloadLifecycleProbe.java:44) and [HotReloadStressRunner.java](/workspace/textd/src/main/java/io/harbor/textd/cli/HotReloadStressRunner.java:21).

Verification: `bin/run-suite` now passes end-to-end, including the 500-cycle constrained soak (`stress-ok cycles=500`), and the lifecycle probe reports the same worker threads reused with `pending_retirements_before_shutdown = 0` and `pending_retirements_after_shutdown = 0`.
